---
title: "Workshop2Module 2"
author: "Dhevin Fernando"
format: html
editor: visual
---

## Quarto

Quarto enables you to weave together content and executable code into a finished document. To learn more about Quarto see <https://quarto.org>.

## 2.2 Tidying data

Loading tidyverse

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
```

## 2.4 Lengthening datasets

```{r}
billboard
billboard |> 
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("wk"), 
    names_to = "week", 
    values_to = "rank"
  )
#remove the NA values
billboard |> 
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("wk"), 
    names_to = "week", 
    values_to = "rank",
    values_drop_na = TRUE
  )

#converting values within week variable from string to numbers
billboard_longer <- billboard |> 
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("wk"), 
    names_to = "week", 
    values_to = "rank",
    values_drop_na = TRUE
  ) |> 
  mutate(
    week = parse_number(week)
  )
billboard_longer

```

```{r}
df <- tribble(
  ~id,  ~bp1, ~bp2,
   "A",  100,  120,
   "B",  140,  115,
   "C",  120,  125
)

df |> 
  pivot_longer(
    cols = bp1:bp2, 
    names_to = "measurement", 
    values_to = "value"
  )|> 
  mutate(
    measurement = parse_number(measurement)
  )
```

## 2.5 Widening datasets

```{r}
cms_patient_experience
cms_patient_experience |> 
  distinct(measure_cd, measure_title)
cms_patient_experience |> 
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = measure_cd,
    values_from = prf_rate
  )
cms_patient_experience |> 
  pivot_wider(
    id_cols = starts_with("org"),
    names_from = measure_cd,
    values_from = prf_rate
  )

```

```{r}
df <- tribble(
  ~id, ~measurement, ~value,
  "A",        "bp1",    100,
  "B",        "bp1",    140,
  "B",        "bp2",    115, 
  "A",        "bp2",    120,
  "A",        "bp3",    105
)
df |> 
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = measurement,
    values_from = value
  )
df |> 
  distinct(measurement) |> 
  pull()
df |> 
  select(-measurement, -value) |> 
  distinct()
df |> 
  select(-measurement, -value) |> 
  distinct() |> 
  mutate(x = NA, y = NA, z = NA)


```

## 2.6 Pivoting penguins

Lengthening:

```{r}
library(palmerpenguins)
penguins

# Create a long version of the penguins dataset

penguins_long <- penguins |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g),
    names_to = "measurement_type",
    values_to = "value"
  )
# View the result
head(penguins_long)

```

```{r}
#plot the graph
penguins_long |>
  drop_na(value) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = value, fill = species)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, alpha = 0.7, colour = "black") +
  facet_wrap( ~ measurement_type, scales = "free_x") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Morphometric distributions across penguin species",
    x = "Measurement value",
    y = "Frequency"
  )

```

Widening:

```{r}
#First, let's calculate the mean body mass for each species on each island using group_by() and summarise().
mass_summary <- penguins |>
  drop_na(body_mass_g) |>
  group_by(species, island) |>
  summarise(mean_mass = mean(body_mass_g))

head(mass_summary)

# now use pivot wider to pull island names into columns for publication readiness
mass_matrix <- mass_summary |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = island,
    values_from = mean_mass
  )
head(mass_matrix)

```

## 2.7 Separating and uniting

```{r}
#SEPERATING
table3 %>% 
  separate(rate, into = c("cases", "population"))
#or
table3 %>% 
  separate(rate, into = c("cases", "population"), sep = "/")
# but we need to convert the seperated columns into integers so...
table3 %>% 
  separate(rate, into = c("cases", "population"), convert = TRUE)
#you can also specify an integet to seperate at i.e positive starts from left and moves right and vice versa
table3 %>% 
  separate(year, into = c("century", "year"), sep = 2)

#UNITING (we define sep as "" to prevent seperators from being added between the joined values)
table5 %>% 
  unite(new, century, year, sep = "")

```

## 2.8 Wrangling strings and dates

```{r}
library(tidyverse)

# A remarkably messy data frame of field sites
messy_sites <- tibble(
  site_id = c(" Nelly Bay", "nelly_bay", "NELLY BAY", " Geoffrey_Bay ", "geoffrey bay")
)
messy_sites
# Using stringr within mutate to standardize the text
clean_sites <- messy_sites |>
  mutate(
    # 1. Convert everything to lowercase
    site_clean = str_to_lower(site_id),
    # 3. Trim any leading or trailing whitespace (invisible spaces at the ends)
    site_clean = str_trim(site_clean),
    # 2. Replace any spaces with underscores
    site_clean = str_replace_all(site_clean, pattern = " ", replacement = "_"),
  )

print(clean_sites)


```

using lubridate

```{r}
library(lubridate)

# Parsing different date formats
date_1 <- dmy("25/12/2026")
date_2 <- ymd("2026-12-25")

# R now recognizes these as identical Date objects
date_1 == date_2

# A tibble of raw sensor data with a messy character timestamp
sensor_data <- tibble(
  raw_time = c("14-05-2026 08:30:00", "14-05-2026 08:45:00", "14-05-2026 09:00:00"),
  temperature = c(24.5, 24.6, 24.4)
)
sensor_data

# Converting character strings to true POSIXct datetime objects
sensor_clean <- sensor_data |>
  mutate(
    true_time = dmy_hms(raw_time)
  )

print(sensor_clean)



```

## 2.9 joining tables

```{r}
# Table 1: Biological observation data
observations <- tibble(
  site_code = c("NB", "GB", "MI", "NB", "HB"),
  species = c("Trout", "Snapper", "Trout", "Cod", "Trout"),
  count = c(5, 2, 1, 3, 8)
)

# Table 2: Spatial metadata
site_metadata <- tibble(
  site_code = c("NB", "GB", "MI", "RP", "WP"),
  zone = c("Marine National Park", "Conservation Park", "Habitat Protection", "General Use", "Other Use"),
  lat = c(-19.16, -19.15, -19.14, -19.12, -19.11)
)
observations
site_metadata

# Joining metadata to our observations
joined_data <- observations |>
  left_join(site_metadata, by = join_by(site_code))

print(joined_data)


#inner join which drops rows that dont have complete matches
matched_data <- observations |>
  inner_join(site_metadata, by = join_by(site_code))
glimpse(matched_data)
print(matched_data)

# Which observations are missing from our metadata dictionary?
missing_context <- observations |>
  anti_join(site_metadata, by = join_by(site_code))

missing_context
```

## 2.10 Missing values

dealing with legacy N/A values (i.e. impossibly high placeholder values)

```{r}
# Raw data from an old temperature logger
logger_data <- tibble(
  depth_m = c(10, 20, 30, 40),
  temp_c = c(24.5, 24.1, -999, 23.5)) # -999 is a known sensor error code

# Convert the -999 error codes to true NA values
fixed_logger <- logger_data |>
  mutate(temp_c = na_if(temp_c, -999))

print(fixed_logger)

```

replacing NAs with zeros or any other value

```{r}
# Simulate some count data
shark_counts <- tibble(
  site = c("Reef_A", "Reef_B", "Reef_C"),
  shark_count = c(3, NA, 5)) # The NA here actually means 0 sharks were seen

# Replace NA with 0 
shark_fixed <- shark_counts |>
  mutate(shark_count = coalesce(shark_count, 0))

print(shark_fixed)

#NaN values
cpue_data <- tibble(
  site = c("Bay_1", "Bay_2"),
  catch = c(10, 0),
  effort_hours = c(2, 0))

# Calculating CPUE (catch / effort)
cpue_calc <- cpue_data |>
  mutate(
    cpue = catch / effort_hours)

print(cpue_calc)


```

implicit missing values:

```{r}
raw_catch <- tibble(
  site = c("Reef_1", "Reef_1", "Reef_2"),
  species = c("Pmaculatus", "Pleopardus", "Pmaculatus"),
  count = c(5, 2, 8))

raw_catch

# Force inclusion of hidden zero-catch data
full_catch_matrix <- raw_catch |>
  complete(site, species, fill = list(count = 0))

print(full_catch_matrix)


```

drop_na()

```{r}
sensor_log <- tibble(
  day = 1:4,
  salinity = c(35.2, 35.1, NA, 35.3)
)

# Remove any row where salinity is missing
clean_log <- sensor_log |>
  drop_na(salinity)

clean_log
```

Exercise 1:

```{r}
# Make sure packages are loaded
library(tidyverse)
library(palmerpenguins)

# The messy metadata provided by your colleague
island_metadata <- tibble(
  island_name = c(" biscoe", "Dream ", "Torgersen"),
  station_install = c("15/01/2003", "22-03-2004", "05/11/2001"),
  latitude = c(-64.81, -64.73, -64.76)
)
penguins
print(island_metadata)

# Using stringr within mutate to standardize the text
clean_metadata <- island_metadata |>
  mutate(
    # 1. Convert everything to Title case
    island_name = str_to_title(island_name),
    # 3. Trim any leading or trailing whitespace (invisible spaces at the ends)
    island_name = str_trim(island_name),
    # 2. Replace any spaces with underscores
    island_name = str_replace_all(island_name, pattern = " ", replacement = "_"),
    #convert dates to true dates with lubridate
    station_install = dmy(station_install)
  )

print(clean_metadata)


```

Exercise 2

```{r}
# Joining metadata to our observations
penguins_spatial <- penguins |>
  left_join(clean_metadata, by = join_by(island == island_name))

print(penguins_spatial)
head(penguins_spatial)
```

Exercise 3

#### **Exercise 3: The Wide Summary Matrix**

A collaborator has asked for a clean, publication-ready table showing the **maximum body mass** recorded for each species, separated by island.

**Task:** Starting with your penguins_spatial dataset:

Drop any missing values in the body_mass_g column.

1.  Group by species and island.

2.  Summarise the data to find the maximum body mass (max()).

3.  Pivot the dataset wider so that the species form the rows, and the islands form the columns.

```{r}
# Remove any row where body_mass is missing
clean_penguins_spatial <- penguins_spatial |>
  drop_na(body_mass_g)
print(clean_penguins_spatial)

#Group by species and island and summarise to find max body mass
clean_penguins_spatial_mass_summary <- clean_penguins_spatial |>
  group_by(species, island) |>
  summarise(maximum_mass = max(body_mass_g))

head(clean_penguins_spatial_mass_summary)

# pivot wider to pull island names into columns and keep species in rows
spatial_max_mass_matrix <- clean_penguins_spatial_mass_summary |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = island,
    values_from = maximum_mass
  )
head(spatial_max_mass_matrix)
```

### **2.12 Keystone exercise: Estuary Fish Survey - Data Rescue Mission**

**The Premise:** You have inherited a project from a former PhD student who was studying the relationship between water quality and predatory fish assemblages along the Ross River Estuary gradient. Unfortunately, their data management was entirely manual, and the files are a disaster.

They left you with four separate datasets:

1.  estuary_catch_log.xlsx: Handwritten field counts of fish species (using common names), spread across multiple spreadsheet tabs.

2.  estuary_metadata.csv: The spatial coordinates and estuary zones for each site.

3.  estuary_sonde_data.csv: High-frequency water quality sensor readings.

4.  species_dictionary.csv: A taxonomic lookup table linking local common names to their accepted scientific names.

Your mission is to write a single, reproducible R script (or .qmd) that ingests this mess, cleans it, joins it into a single master dataset, and produces a publication-ready visualisation of the estuary's ecology.

Note: the datasets are now available in the [class repository](https://github.com/MB-5370/R4MarineScience)

#### **Deliverables (to include in ePortfolio report/page)**

At the end of this exercise, your final script/document must successfully output:

1.  **A clean master dataset** containing no legacy errors (-999.0), standardised text, a complete zero-catch framework, and scientific names instead of common names.

2.  **A statistical summary table** displaying the mean and standard deviation of fish counts and salinity for each species per zone.

3.  **A multi-panel plot** (faceted by species) showing fish abundance along the salinity gradient, formatted to professional publication standards.

#### **Rules of Engagement**

Because this is an open-ended task, there is no single "correct" way to approach this and structure your code. However, think about all the data-wrangling and visualisation skills you have developed over recent weeks, and adhere to the following professional standards:

-   **Monitor your AI agents:** You are encouraged to use AI to help you write code. However, for every major data wrangling step, make sure you understand what is going on and are not just running AI-written code blindly.

-   **Annotate your work:** To help you with the above, make sure you include comments (#) explaining what the code is doing. If you used an AI prompt to generate a complex block of code, paste your prompt as a comment above it.

1.  **Utilise the tidyverse:** There are many ways of wrangling data, but here we are looking for you to use your growing skills in dplyr, tidyr, stringr, and lubridate functions linked by the pipe (\|\> or %\>%).

-   **Reproducible workflow:** Make sure you stay on top of your PC → Git → GitHub lifecycle, by utilising: save, commit, pull, push!

Load Packages

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(readxl)
```

Load estuary_catch_log.xlsx (main dataset)

```{r}
catch_log1 <- read_excel(here::here("data/estuary_catch_log.xlsx"), sheet = "Lower_ross")
catch_log2 <- read_excel(here::here("data/estuary_catch_log.xlsx"), sheet = "Mid_Ross ")
catch_log3 <- read_excel(here::here("data/estuary_catch_log.xlsx"), sheet = "ROSS MOUTH")
catch_log4 <- read_excel(here::here("data/estuary_catch_log.xlsx"), sheet = "Upper Ross")

catch_log1
catch_log2
catch_log3
catch_log4
```

Bind the dataframes together

prompt used: how do i bind the following dataframes together, ensuring they share the same fields and columns

```{r}
combined_catch_log <- bind_rows(catch_log1, catch_log2, catch_log3, catch_log4)
combined_catch_log

#check how many species and sites are present
combined_catch_log |> 
  distinct(site)

combined_catch_log |> 
  distinct(species)

#clear unnecessary data from workspace
rm(catch_log1, catch_log2, catch_log3, catch_log4)
```

Standardise site and species names and converting dates from strings to date-time

```{r}
# Using stringr within mutate to standardize the text
catch_clean <- combined_catch_log |>
  mutate(
    # 1. Convert everything to lower case for site name
    site = str_to_lower(site),
    # 2. Trim any leading or trailing whitespace (invisible spaces at the ends) for site name
    site = str_trim(site),
    # 3. Replace any spaces with underscores site name
    site = str_replace_all(site, pattern = " ", replacement = "_"),
    # 1. Convert everything to lower case for species
    species = str_to_lower(species),
    # 2. Trim any leading or trailing whitespace (invisible spaces at the ends) for species
    species = str_trim(species),
    # 3. Replace any spaces with underscores for species
    species = str_replace_all(species, pattern = " ", replacement = "_"),
     #convert dates to true dates with lubridate
    date = ymd(date)
  ) |>  # Convert all chr to fct
  mutate(across(where(is.character), as_factor)
  )

print(catch_clean)

#using distinct to check species again

catch_clean |> 
  distinct(site)

catch_clean |> 
  distinct(species)
```

Load metadata

```{r}
estuary_metadata <- read_csv(here::here("data/estuary_metadata.csv"))
sonde_data <- read_csv(here::here("data/estuary_sonde_data.csv"))
species_dictionary <- read_csv(here::here("data/species_dictionary.csv"))
```

standardise metadata

```{r}
# Using stringr within mutate to standardize the text
estuary_metadata_clean <- estuary_metadata |>
  mutate(
    # 1. Convert everything to lower case for site name
    site_name = str_to_lower(site_name),
    # 2. Trim any leading or trailing whitespace (invisible spaces at the ends) for site name
    site_name = str_trim(site_name),
    # 3. Replace any spaces with underscores site name
    site_name = str_replace_all(site_name, pattern = " ", replacement = "_")
    
  ) |>  # Convert all chr to fct
  mutate(across(where(is.character), as_factor)
  )
estuary_metadata_clean
```

Fix dates in sonde and fix NA values

```{r}
sonde_data
# Convert the -999 error codes to true NA values
fixed_sonde <- sonde_data |>
  mutate(turbidity = na_if(turbidity, -999.0),
         timestamp = dmy_hm(timestamp),
         salinity = ifelse(salinity < 0, NA, salinity)#convert negatuve salinity to NA
         ) |>  # Convert all chr to fct
  mutate(across(where(is.character), as_factor)
  )

print(fixed_sonde)

```

fix species dictionary

```{r}
species_dictionary
fixed_species <- species_dictionary |>
  mutate(
    # 1. Convert everything to lower case for site name
    common_name = str_to_lower(common_name),
    # 2. Trim any leading or trailing whitespace (invisible spaces at the ends) for site name
    common_name = str_trim(common_name),
    # 3. Replace any spaces with underscores site name
    common_name = str_replace_all(common_name, pattern = " ", replacement = "_")
    
  ) |>  # Convert all chr to fct
  mutate(across(where(is.character), as_factor)
  )
fixed_species


```

Phase 2:

summarise sensor data (note that you might need to change the posixct variable type across the board

```{r}
daily_sonde_summary <- fixed_sonde |>
  mutate(
    date = as_date(floor_date(timestamp, "day")) #round timestaps to days and set it to date format
  ) |>
  group_by(site, date) |>
  summarize(
    mean_temperature = mean(temperature, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_salinity = mean(salinity, na.rm = TRUE),
     mean_turbidity = mean(turbidity, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

daily_sonde_summary

```

merge species_dictionary with main data

```{r}
merge_catch <- catch_clean |>
  left_join(fixed_species, by = join_by(species == common_name)) |>
  rename(common_name = species) #rename the column containing common names previously named "species" to an appropriate heading


print(merge_catch)

```

merge with sensor data and spatial data

```{r}
full_merge_catch <- merge_catch |>
  left_join(estuary_metadata_clean, by = join_by(site == site_name)) |>
  left_join(daily_sonde_summary, by = join_by(site, date))
full_merge_catch
```

Phase 3: Zero data framework

Use tidyr::complete() to build a zero-data framework. Ensure that every species is represented at every site, on every sampling day.

-   Replace the resulting NA values in the catch column with 0 using coalesce().

```{r}
# Force inclusion of hidden zero-catch data
finalised_catch <- full_merge_catch |>
  complete(
    nesting( site, date, 
            lat, lon, 
            zone, mean_temperature, mean_salinity, mean_turbidity),
    scientific_name)|>
  mutate(
    # Convert the resulting NA counts to 0
    count = coalesce(count, 0)
  )

print(finalised_catch)

#prompt solution
#finalised_catch <- full_merge_catch %>%
#  complete(
 #   site,
 #   date,
 #   scientific_name, # Use the comprehensive list of species
  #  fill = list(count = 0) # Fill the 'count' column with 0 for missing combinations
 # ) %>%
  # For environmental columns (zone, mean_temp, mean_salinity, mean_turbidity)
  # which are associated with 'site' and 'date', we want to propagate their values
  # for newly created rows that share the same site and date.
  # This assumes these environmental values are consistent for a given site-date.
 # group_by(site, date) %>%
 # fill(zone, mean_temperature, mean_salinity, mean_turbidity, .direction = "downup") %>%
 # ungroup() 
#finalised_catch
```

**Phase 4: Statistical Extraction** From your master dataset, generate a clean summary table showing the mean ± standard error of both the fish catch and the salinity for each scientific species across each estuary zone.

Salinity summary table:

```{r}
#create a summary table showing the mean ± standard error of salinity for each estuary zone using the finalised_catch dataset
salinity_summary_table <- finalised_catch |>
  group_by(zone) |>
  summarise(
    median_salinity = median(mean_salinity, na.rm = TRUE),
    se_salinity = sd(mean_salinity, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n()),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
print(salinity_summary_table)

#| fig-cap: "This figure shows the Salinity Gradient across zones at the Ross River Estuary System at Townsville, NQ, Australia during May of 2026."
#create a bar graph showing the median salinity for each zone with error bars representing the standard error
ggplot(salinity_summary_table, aes(x = zone, y = median_salinity)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "steelblue") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = median_salinity - se_salinity, 
                    ymax = median_salinity + se_salinity), 
                width = 0.2) +
  labs(title = "Median Salinity by Estuary Zone",
       subtitle = "Error bars represent standard error",
       y = "Median Salinity (ppt)",
       x = "Estuary Zone") +
  theme_minimal()

```

Turbidity summary table:

```{r}
#create a summary table showing the mean ± standard error of turbidity for each estuary zone using the finalised_catch dataset
turbidity_summary_table <- finalised_catch |>
  group_by(zone) |>
  summarise(
    median_turbidity = median(mean_turbidity, na.rm = TRUE),
    se_turbidity = sd(mean_turbidity, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n()),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
turbidity_summary_table

#create a bar graph showing the median turbidity for each zone with error bars representing the standard error
turbidity_summary_table|> ggplot(aes(x = zone, y = median_turbidity)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "steelblue") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = median_turbidity - se_turbidity, 
                    ymax = median_turbidity + se_turbidity), 
                width = 0.2) +
  labs(title = "Median Turbidity by Estuary Zone",
       subtitle = "Error bars represent standard error",
       y = "Median Turbidity (NTU)",
       x = "Estuary Zone") +
  theme_minimal()
```

Temperature summary table:

```{r}
#create a summary table showing the mean ± standard error of temperature for each estuary zone using the finalised_catch dataset
temp_summary_table <- finalised_catch |>
  group_by(zone) |>
  summarise(
    median_temperature = median(mean_temperature, na.rm = TRUE),
    se_temperature = sd(mean_temperature, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n()),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
temp_summary_table

#create a bar graph showing the median temperature for each zone with error bars representing the standard error
temp_summary_table|> ggplot(aes(x = zone, y = median_temperature)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "steelblue") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = median_temperature - se_temperature, 
                    ymax = median_temperature + se_temperature), 
                width = 0.2) +
  labs(title = "Median Temperature by Estuary Zone",
       subtitle = "Error bars represent standard error",
       y = "Median Temperature (°C)",
       x = "Estuary Zone") +
  theme_minimal()
```

This is my attempt to plot fish abundance with salinity as there are notable differences between the salinities at each estuary zone:

```{r}
#create a summary table for fish catch showing the mean ± standard error of fish catch for each scientific species across each estuary zone using the finalised_catch dataset
fish_summary_table <- finalised_catch |>
  group_by(scientific_name, zone) |>
  summarise(
    mean_count = mean(count, na.rm = TRUE),
    se_count = sd(count, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n()),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
print(fish_summary_table)

#my plot attempt

#plot mean fish count as a line graph for each scientific species across each estuary zone with error bars representing the standard error, faceted by species. The plot will also include jittered data points for count from the finalised_catch dataset, with points coloured by zone and sized by mean salinity. The lines, points and error bars for mean_count will be rendered in black to ensure they are visible against the coloured points representing the raw data.
ggplot(fish_summary_table, aes(x = zone, y = mean_count, fill= zone, group = scientific_name)) +
  geom_line(color = "black") +
  geom_point(data = finalised_catch, aes(x = zone, y = count, colour = zone, size = mean_salinity), position = position_jitter(width = 0.2), alpha = 0.2) + geom_point(position = "dodge", size = 4, pch = 23) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_count - se_count, ymax = mean_count + se_count), width = 0.2, color = "black") +
  facet_wrap(~ scientific_name) +
  labs(title = "Predatory Fish Abundance across Estuarine Salinity Gradients",
       subtitle = "Ross River Estuary System, May 2026",
       y = "Abundance (per day)",
       x = "Spatial Zone") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "right")



```

Based on lecturer plot instructions this is what the plot would look like:

```{r}
#based off bens plot
fish_summary_table |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = zone, y = mean_count, fill = zone)) +
  geom_line(group = 1) +
  # add in points from master data
  geom_point(data = finalised_catch, aes(x = zone, y = count), 
             position = position_jitter(width = 0.2), alpha = 0.2, size = 1, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  geom_point(position = "dodge", size = 4, pch = 23) +
  
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_count - se_count, ymax = mean_count + se_count), 
                position = position_dodge(0.9), width = 0.2) +
  
  facet_wrap(~ scientific_name, scales = "fixed") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(face = "italic", size = 10, colour = "grey20"),
   # legend.position = "bottom"
    legend.position = "none"
  ) +
  labs(
    #title = "Predatory Fish Abundance across Estuarine Salinity Gradients",
    #subtitle = "Ross River Estuary System, May 2026",
    x = "Spatial Zone",
    y = "Abundance (per day)",
    fill = "Spatial Zone"
  )
```
